title: Ma famille
description: Name your family members and use possessive adjectives correctly — a frequent source of mistakes for English speakers, and a classic TEF/TCF reading topic.

content.subtitle: Module 2 · Leçon 1
content.body: Talking about family is one of the most common topics in everyday French conversation — and in the TEF/TCF exam. Let's learn the vocabulary and the tricky possessive adjectives that go with it.

content.exampleSentence: Mon père s'appelle Michel et ma mère s'appelle Anne.
content.nativeExample: My father is called Michel and my mother is called Anne.

content.exampleSentence: J'ai un frère et une sœur.
content.nativeExample: I have one brother and one sister.

content.explanation: A few more essential family words you'll need to describe your whole family tree.

The #1 mistake English speakers make: French possessive adjectives agree with the GENDER OF THE NOUN they describe, not the gender of the owner. "Son père" can mean "his father" OR "her father" — you only know from context.

examples.text: mon frère / ma sœur / mes parents
examples.translation: my brother / my sister / my parents

examples.text: ton frère / ta sœur / tes parents
examples.translation: your brother / your sister / your parents (informal)

examples.text: son frère / sa sœur / ses parents
examples.translation: his/her brother / his/her sister / his/her parents

examples.text: notre frère / notre sœur / nos parents
examples.translation: our brother / our sister / our parents

examples.text: votre frère / votre sœur / vos parents
examples.translation: your brother / your sister / your parents (formal/plural)

examples.text: leur frère / leur sœur / leurs parents
examples.translation: their brother / their sister / their parents

content.body: Before a feminine noun starting with a vowel sound, French uses "mon/ton/son" instead of "ma/ta/sa" — purely for pronunciation. Example: "mon amie" (my friend, feminine), not "ma amie".

content.prompt: ___ sœur est médecin. (Talking about your own sister)
Quel membre de la famille entendez-vous ?

cards.title: Associez le mot à sa traduction

content.prompt: Match each family word to its English meaning.
Arrange the words to say "My parents have three children."

cards.title: Quels mots décrivent la famille proche ?

content.prompt: Select every word for immediate family (parents, siblings, children) — not extended family.

lines.speaker: Candidat
lines.text: J'ai un frère et une sœur.
lines.translation: I have one brother and one sister.

lines.speaker: Candidat
lines.text: Mon père est professeur et ma mère est infirmière.
lines.translation: My father is a teacher and my mother is a nurse.

content.body: "Parlez-moi de votre famille" is one of the most common TEF/TCF oral prompts. Prepare 3–4 sentences about your family in advance — it almost always comes up.

content.prompt: Repeat this short family description out loud.
content.targetText: J'ai un frère et une sœur. Mon père est professeur et ma mère est infirmière.

content.intro: Two quick questions before we move on.

questions.prompt: "Mon" s'utilise devant "amie" (féminin, commence par une voyelle).

content.heading: Key takeaways
points: Core family vocabulary: père, mère, frère, sœur, parents, enfants, and extended family
Possessive adjectives (mon/ma/mes...) agree with the noun's gender, not the owner's
"Mon/ton/son" replace "ma/ta/sa" before a feminine noun starting with a vowel sound
"Parlez-moi de votre famille" is a near-guaranteed TEF/TCF oral prompt

content.title: Leçon terminée !
content.message: Bravo ! You can now talk about your entire family with the right possessive adjectives.
